analogy: Think of "Understanding Social Institutions" through the chapter's own opening images: 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY CHAPTER 3 UNDERSTANDING SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS I INTRODUCTION This book began with a discussion about the interaction of the individual and society. We saw that each of us as individuals, occupies a place or location in society. Each one of us has a... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Understanding Social Institutions is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY CHAPTER 3 UNDERSTANDING SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS I INTRODUCTION This book began with a discussion about the interaction of the individual and society.

The text explains that we saw that each of us as individuals, occupies a place or location in society.

The text explains that each one of us has a status and a role or roles, but these are not simply what we as individuals choose.

The text explains that they are not like roles a film actor may or may not opt to do.

The text explains that there are social institutions that constrain and control, punish and reward.

The text explains that they could be ‘macro’ social institutions like the state or ‘micro’ ones like the family.

The text explains that here in this chapter we are introduced to social institutions, and also to how sociology/social anthropology studies them.

The text explains that this chapter puts forth a very brief idea of some of the central areas where important social institutions are located namely: (i) family, marriage and kinship; (ii) politics; (iii) economics; (iv) religion; and (v) education.

The text explains that in the broadest sense, an institution is something that works according to rules established or at least acknowledged by law or by custom.

The text explains that and whose regular and continuous operation cannot be understood without taking those rules into account.

The text explains that institutions impose constraints on individuals.

The text explains that they also provide him/her with opportunities.

The text explains that an institution can also be viewed as an end in itself.

The text explains that indeed people have viewed family, religion, state or even education as an end in itself.

The text explains that we have already seen that there are conflicting and different understandings of concepts within sociology.

The text explains that we have also been introduced to the functionalist and conflict perspective, and seen how differently they saw the same thing, for instance, stratification or social control.

The text explains that family and kinship are thus subject to change and transformation due to macro economic processes but the direction of change need not always be similar for all countries and regions.

The text explains that (Chanana 1993:WS26) Rock-a-bye-baby, combs in your pretty hair, The bridegroom will come soon and take you away The drums beat loudly, the shehnai is playing softly A stranger’s son has come to fetch me Come my playmates, come with our toys Let us play, for I shall never play again When I go off to the strangers’ house.

The text explains that (Dube 2001: 94) Defining Some Basic Concepts, Particularly those of Family, Kinship and Marriage A family is a group of persons directly linked by kin connections,

The text explains that much of the work done in the informal economy, for example, is not recorded in any direct way in the official employment statistics.

The text explains that the term ‘informal economy’ refers to transactions outside the sphere of regular employment, sometimes involving the exchange of cash for services provided, but also often involving the direct exchange of goods or services.

The text explains that any serious agitation for a rise in wages would lead manufacturers to shift their operations to other localities, beyond the reach of unionists... whether it is the payment of the existing minimum wage, or its substantial revision upwards, what is important is to enlist the support of the retailer in order to create the necessary pressure upon the government and local agencies for a higher wage structure and its effective implementation.

The text explains that education should prepare the child for a special occupation, and enable the child to internalise the core values of society.

The text explains that discuss the visual Activity 13 A study of a kindergarten suggested that children learn that: • ‘work activities are more important than play activities’. • ‘work includes any and all teacher-directed activities.’ • ‘work is compulsory and free time activities are called play’ (Apple 1979:102).

The text explains that rAO, M.S.A., An Urban Sociology in India : Reader and Source Book.

The text explains that 1974. ‘Changing Role of Religious Specialists in Nasik — The Pilgrim City’, in ed.

The text explains that tiny’s Granny in Contemporary Indian Short Stories; Series 1.

The text explains that anthropological Explorations in Gender : Intersecting Fields.

The text explains that social Implications of Schooling : Knowledge, Pedagogy and Consciousness.

The text explains that 62 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY ROY CHOUDHURY , SUPRIYA .

The text explains that 2005. ‘Labour Activism and Women in the Unorganised Sector : Garment Export Industry in Bangalore’, Economic and Political Weekly.

The text explains that social Change in India : Crisis and Resilience.

The text explains that 2002. ‘Family, Kinship and Marriage in India’, in Student’s Britannica, India.

The text explains that vol.6, Encyclopedia Britannica Private Ltd, New Delhi, pp.145-155.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology, Chapter 3: Understanding Social Institutions

cases.name: In India
cases.point: In India studies however suggest that families need not become nuclear in an industrial pattern of economy (Singh 1993: 83). (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: Variation
cases.point: Variation in Family Forms A central debate in India has been about the shift from nuclear family to joint families. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 2)

cases.name: The
cases.point: The fact is that nuclear families have always existed in India particularly among deprived castes and classes. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 2)

cases.name: Shah
cases.point: Shah remarks that in post-independent India the joint family has steadily increased. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 2)

cases.name: The
cases.point: The contributing factor is the increasing life expectancy in India according to him. (NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology Ch3 pg 2)

schemes.name: No

prelims_traps: Do not treat 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY and CHAPTER 3 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Understanding Social Institutions; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Understanding Social Institutions through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 40 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY and CHAPTER 3 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
